text: The surgeon identifies the splenic artery using fluorescence and clamps it with a bulldog clamp at the beginning of a spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy. This reduces blood inflow to the splenic vessels, minimizing bleeding during dissection. The surgeon then begins dissecting to separate the spleen vessels from the pancreas, navigating through strong adhesions and numerous spleen and vein branches close to the spleen.

The surgeon uses the splenic artery first approach to control bleeding by clamping the splenic artery with a bulldog clamp, reducing blood inflow to the splenic vessels. Once bleeding is controlled, the clamp is removed.
